title: [Bug]: Podman commands sometimes run for extremely long time
body:
### Issue Description

`podman ps` with four running containers sometimes takes about half a minute to return with the output in Powershell.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Install Podman on Windows
2. Run some containers
3. Run `podman ps`


### Describe the results you received

The results of `podman ps` only show up after about half a minute.

### Describe the results you expected

I expect to see the results almost instantly.

### podman info output


comments.body:
Can you be more specific with your reproducer? How many container are you running. Are the containers being start/stopped during the ps command? The ps command needs to get a lock for each container so when you have other podman commands running during this it might need to wait  some time until the lock becomes available.

Also if that happens please check the podman service log (`journalctl --unit podman.service`), use `podman machine ssh` to enter the VM. You can also test if the podman ps command executed in the VM also hangs.
@Luap99 I will try your suggestions, thanks. I just ran four containers when the issue occurred.

Here's the `journalctl` output after I 
- started an `activemq` container in interactive shell
- ended it with `CTRL+C`
- issued `podman ps` in the same Powershell terminal
- after around half a minute issued `podman image list` in another Powershell terminal
- waited for half a minute or longer until `podman image list` returned
- waited for a few minutes until `podman ps` returned

[podman.log](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/10511783/podman.log)

After `podman ps` finally returned the next try returned as quickly as expected.

Couldn't see anything of interest there:

> podman ps -a --log-level trace

A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 Any update on this?

@Luap99 hmm can't think of any Windows behavior that would cause it. Seems the lock scenario you are thinking of is the likely culprit?

@biergit does this happen for you if you run the podman commands on the WSL prompt instead of powershell?
@n1hility Yes it also happened there. If this is only happening to me I would blame the corporate environment my Laptop runs in. I haven't checked it on my private device.

I've been having a lot of issues with podman responsiveness on mac (m1). I use vscode remote containers and about 2 or 3 times a day it will lose connection for a little bit when podman hangs. In my case podman ps took a bit less than 2 minutes to return. ``` $ time podman ps CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES d0cf856bbe2b lscr.io/linuxserver/mariadb:latest 4 weeks ago Up 19 hours 0.0.0.0:3306->3306/tcp mariadb b655e3f23ac8 localhost/vsc-dev-49473a048bbb407af5bb49a276fa8492:latest -c echo Container... 6 days ago Up 19 hours 0.0.0.0:8443->8080/tcp quizzical_chatelet real 1m41.170s user 0m0.025s sys 0m0.015s ``` Also maybe unrelated but at one point gvproxy was consuming ~ 8 GB of memory. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/65360454/224179566-79133179-4126-4eee-9551-c85006b34a9d.png) Above was captured at a random point in time, I wish I checked before/after podman hung to see if there was any significant difference it was nowhere near above after one of these unresponsive periods. I only run 2 containers podman: 4.4.1
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@vrothberg PTAL
This looks like somethings you might have fixed.

> @vrothberg PTAL This looks like somethings you might have fixed.

Commit de84be54ebce is part of Podman v4.4.1, so the ps-performance optimizations did not help there.

Listing three containers should _never_ take that long, so some other evil must be at work here.

@benoitf, have you seen this symptom on the Podman-Desktop side?

usually I got slow response on listing something when there is an external process performing tasks like pulling images (or doing other actions in parallel) (There are some locks)
but if the containers are there and no external queries, no it's immediate

Could be interesting if not already asked/got the status of all the lock in the VM (with the kill command)

and to run the command inside the VM instead of the host
@vrothberg @benoitf @biergit Is this still an issue?
On heavy load I have this kind of issues where REST API is not able to respond quickly
Maybe it's worth investigating whether this issue is related to #20379 (and by extension https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/10688).

I have maybe something as well on Debian GNU/Linux AMD64 with Podman 4.9.3.

I would say it takes approx. 10 seconds. Not huge, but not "immediate" either IMHO.

Approx. 30-40 Containers running but none is doing anything at the moment (just trying out some things).

I've got a related situation where a container takes a long time to start (about 20min.). During this time, `podman ps` will hang indefinitely with no output, until said container has finished its startup. Maybe related, maybe not.
Im having the same issue: when a container starts and it takes a long time, it's basically impossible to execute any podman command. This should be non-blocking.

I've got a working hypothesis that maybe it has something to do with SELinux relabeling. Because it only happens to me with one particular container, and this container happens to be a file server with a multi-terabyte bind mount with `:z` option. So I suspect it's literally the recursive descent and relabeling of a bajillion files (especially on a slower HDD array) that is blocking execution. 

Maybe this is something I can help investigate when I got time, but I figured it wouldn't hurt to just throw the idea out here for now.
Yup the :z would cause that.  You might consider mounting the volumen with the bajillion files with a context mount, and then removing the :z

> Yup the :z would cause that.  You might consider mounting the volumen with the bajillion files with a context mount, and then removing the :z 
> 

Music to my ears! It's the first time I've heard about a context mount so I don't really know what it is, but I'll investigate tomorrow. Thanks for the recommendation.

`man mount`, you can mount an entire file system with a single SELinux label.

```
  context=context, fscontext=context, defcontext=context, and
       rootcontext=context
           The context= option is useful when mounting filesystems that do not
           support extended attributes, such as a floppy or hard disk formatted
           with VFAT, or systems that are not normally running under SELinux,
           such as an ext3 or ext4 formatted disk from a non-SELinux
           workstation. You can also use context= on filesystems you do not
           trust, such as a floppy. It also helps in compatibility with
           xattr-supporting filesystems on earlier 2.4.<x> kernel versions.
           Even where xattrs are supported, you can save time not having to
           label every file by assigning the entire disk one security context.

           A commonly used option for removable media is
           context="system_u:object_r:removable_t.

...
```

Basically mount with `system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0` then all of the container processes can read/write the content.

You could also do container_file_ro_t if you want readonly access.

Finally you can run containers without SELinux separation with --security-opt label=unconfined

I recently wrote this article:

https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2025/04/11/my-advice-selinux-container-labeling

Thanks for the excellent article, it was a great read. The colouring book is amazing too, loved it 😆

I see what you mean by a context mount now. Although, labelling the entire filesystem is not suitable for my particular use case.

My ZFS dataset is mounted on `/mnt/data`, and it's shared by multiple services, not all of which are containers. The host path of the bind mount for this container is actually `/mnt/data/foo`. So I'm thinking, maybe I'll just `semanage fcontext -at container_file_t '/mnt/data/foo(/.*)?'` + `restorecon` instead. This would work too I'm guessing?
Yes.

I don't agree that it's always SELinux related, as what I experienced on Debian 8 Months ago cannot be due to SELinux since Debian doesn't have/use SELinux. I think it can be triggered by many Things, apparently SELinux being one of them, at least for @cyqsimon Situation.

I'm sure that NOT the Case in all Circumstances though ...

> I think it can be triggered by many Things, apparently SELinux being one of them, at least for @cyqsimon Situation.

I agree with this assessment. This is just one possible reason for this very general symptom.

There are two situations where starting a container can take a long time, SELinux, as well as modifications of the User Namespace.  For example in rootless containers running something like --userns=keep-id which cause a recursive chown of all files in the container image.

Other issues of slowdown are usually network related, as most things are. Bad DNS, network flood, updating HUGE images ...

> My ZFS dataset is mounted on `/mnt/data`, and it's shared by multiple services, not all of which are containers. The host path of the bind mount for this container is actually `/mnt/data/foo`. So I'm thinking, maybe I'll just `semanage fcontext -at container_file_t '/mnt/data/foo(/.*)?'` + `restorecon` instead. This would work too I'm guessing?

I'm happy to report back that this did work. I also employed MCS labels to achieve equivalent security to `:Z`, which does please my perfectionist brain. My container startup time is now under 1 second, as expected for this server and this application.

Although when I think more about it: although it makes sense that relabeling such a large bind mount would inevitably take a long time, and hence what I did before is most certainly a configuration error (or at minimum, a case of sub-optimal configuration), it's also sensible to say that podman should *account for* this possibility. After all, blocking the terminal for no obvious reason is not a great user experience.

By "account for", I'm thinking of the following possibilities:

1. Print a warning to the terminal after a dozen seconds informing the user that relabeling may take a while.
2. Do relabeling asynchronously: container transitions to a "preparing" state (which precedes the "starting" state) while relabeling is happening, and `podman` exits with `0` as long as the task is set up.

Idea 2 sounds like it will involve some architectural changes so probably not very practical. But maybe idea 1 is something worth thinking about?

---

### Edit:

It's also important to note that when SELinux relabeling is in progress, not only does the `podman` command that caused the relabeling hang, so do all other completely unrelated `podman` invocations, as far as I've tested. So maybe it's not just a simple case of synchronous blocking within podman, instead I fear it may actually be caused by a blocking kernel interface of some sort. Hence idea 1 may be insufficient, especially in multi-user environments. But again, this is all just educated speculation.
